sections.heading: Performance Evaluation
sections.body: Evaluating an AI system means judging it against criteria relevant to its actual task — not just whether it eventually produces an answer, but how good that answer is, how reliably it's produced, and at what cost in time and resources.

sections.heading: Accuracy
sections.body: Accuracy measures how often a system's outputs are correct. It's a useful measure but not always sufficient on its own — for example, a system correct 95% of the time can still be dangerously unreliable if its rare failures are severe.

sections.heading: Completeness
sections.body: A search algorithm is complete if it's guaranteed to find a solution whenever one exists. Incomplete algorithms might miss valid solutions in some cases — for example, by exploring one branch of a search space forever without ever backtracking to check others.

sections.heading: Optimality
sections.body: A search algorithm is optimal if it's guaranteed to find the lowest-cost (best) solution among all possible solutions, not merely any solution. An algorithm can be complete without being optimal — it might reliably find a solution, but not always the cheapest one.

sections.heading: Time Complexity
sections.body: Time complexity describes how the running time of an algorithm grows as the size of the problem (e.g. the size of the state space) increases — usually expressed in terms like the number of nodes generated during search.

sections.heading: Space Complexity
sections.body: Space complexity describes how much memory an algorithm requires as the problem size grows — this matters because some search strategies that are fast in time can require storing an impractically large number of states in memory.

quiz.question: A search algorithm that always finds a solution when one exists is called:
What does optimality guarantee that mere completeness does not?
options: That a solution exists at all
That the lowest-cost solution is found, not just any solution

quiz.question: Space complexity primarily describes:
options: How much memory an algorithm needs as problem size grows
The physical size of the hardware
